_meta.note: BuzzFeed quiz tone · curly apostrophes only · glossary locked

title_001: The Stranger Dopamine Master Quiz · Albert Camus
html_001: Albert Camus · 1942 · A classic of absurdist literature
html_002: The story of a man who won’t even perform his grief
html_005: — the opening line of The Stranger
html_006: Master Meursault’s story — the novel’s characters, ideas, and themes — through
html_008:
.
        You start easy and it steadily gets tougher —
html_009: wrong answers keep coming back until you nail them.
html_017: Into the world of the sun →
html_026: That’s the end of your free preview
html_029: You saw right through the absurd
html_042: and you’ll never forget it. The root of it all is
html_043: the meaninglessness of the world and Meursault’s indifference
html_047: A world that answers with no meaning
html_049: 😶 Refusing to fake feeling (a blank face at the funeral)
html_050: He won’t act out what he doesn’t feel
html_052: ☀️ A chance killing, pushed by sun and heat
html_055: ⚖️ A trial that judges the ‘crime of not crying,’ not the murder
html_058: 🌊 A death sentence — and a man embracing the gentle indifference of the world
html_059: “I opened myself to the gentle indifference of the world”
html_061: Table of contents at a glance
html_065: Focused review of what you missed
html_067: goes deeper into the characters, themes, and symbols of The Stranger, chapter by chapter.
html_073: A repeat master quiz that locks a book’s core in with dopamine
html_074: This app is secondary learning content that reworks the book’s key ideas for study. For the full reading experience, pick up the original.

js_004: Which French writer wrote The Stranger?
js_009: The Stranger is a novel published in 1942 by the French writer Albert Camus. Camus won the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1957.
js_011: In what year did this novel first appear in France?
js_016: The Stranger came out from Gallimard in 1942, in the thick of World War II. It was Camus’s first novel.
js_018: What is the name of the protagonist who carries this novel from start to finish?
js_023: The protagonist is Meursault, an ordinary office clerk living in Algiers. The whole story is told flatly, in his first-person voice.
js_025: In which city, where Meursault lives, is this novel set?
js_030: The setting is Algiers, a port city in French Algeria. The scorching Mediterranean sun and sea stay a key backdrop throughout the novel.
js_032: What news does the famous opening line, “Maman died today,” deliver?
js_037: The novel opens with “Maman died today. Or maybe yesterday.” Announcing a death yet unsure of the very date, the line distills Meursault’s indifference.
js_039: The day after his mother’s funeral, which woman does Meursault meet at the beach and start seeing?
js_044: Marie Cardona is a former coworker of Meursault’s. They meet again at the sea the day after the funeral, swim together, watch a comedy, and become lovers.
js_046: Which man on the same floor asks Meursault to write a letter for him?
js_047: Old Salamano, who keeps a dog
js_048: Masson, owner of the seaside cabin
js_050: Raymond Sintès is Meursault’s neighbor on the same floor. Getting mixed up with this ill-reputed man later counts against Meursault at the trial.
js_052: Which old neighbor always abuses his aging dog, yet grieves when it goes missing?
js_056: Old Salamano curses and beats his sick dog, but once it runs off he sobs all night. His tangle of love and hate strangely mirrors Meursault.
js_058: In Part One, where does the decisive event that changes Meursault’s fate take place?
js_059: A blazing, sun-scorched beach at noon
js_063: It happens on a beach drenched in blinding sun. In the searing heat, Meursault does something he can never take back.
js_065: Whom does Meursault shoot dead with a revolver on the beach?
js_066: A nameless Arab man on the beach
js_068: Masson, his friend at the beach
js_070: The man Meursault shoots is an Arab who is never even named. That anonymity later becomes a major point of debate around the novel.
js_081: Albert Camus (1913–1960) grew up poor in French Algeria. Through The Stranger, The Plague, and The Myth of Sisyphus he unfolded his philosophy of the absurd, and at 44 he won the Nobel Prize in Literature.
js_082: The Stranger, Camus’s first novel, was published by Gallimard in 1942. Sartre’s essay helped make it famous, and it is now a classic translated into more than 100 languages.
js_083: Meursault is an ordinary office clerk in Algiers. His refusal to dress up his feelings — saying only what he sees and feels — is the starting point of the novel’s questions.
js_084: The setting is Algiers in French Algeria. The blazing Mediterranean sun, sea, and sand rule the characters’ senses and actions — practically another protagonist.
js_085: The opening line, ‘Maman died today. Or maybe yesterday,’ ranks among the most famous in world literature. Even facing a death, he can’t be sure of the date — his indifference shows at once.
js_086: Marie Cardona, a former coworker of Meursault’s, meets him again at the sea the day after the funeral and becomes his lover. Bright and full of life, she stands in contrast to his indifference.
js_087: Raymond Sintès is an ill-reputed neighbor. That Meursault kept his company and even wrote a letter for him becomes the pretext for branding him ‘bad company’ at the trial.
js_088: Old Salamano abuses his sick dog yet grieves once he loses it. His tangle of love and hate is another portrait of a person who can’t show feeling honestly.
js_089: The decisive event unfolds on a dazzling beach. What makes this novel singular is that not grief or hatred but the physical sensations of sun and heat drive the act.
js_090: The man Meursault shoots is a nameless ‘Arab.’ Depicting a native of colonial Algeria as anonymous is exactly what later led postcolonial critics to reread the novel.
js_096: The gentle indifference of the world
js_105: What moves Meursault isn’t ideology or emotion but sensation. The light stabbing his eyes and the heat pull the trigger.
js_107: He won’t fake sadness or love. This refusal to dress up feelings he doesn’t have is what makes him a stranger.
js_109: The mismatch between a human craving meaning and a world that offers none. This is the core philosophy the novel gives shape to.
js_111: Meursault is condemned not for the murder but for not crying at the funeral. Attitude, more than action, becomes the ground for guilt.
js_113: Camus called this Meursault’s crime. He merely declined to perform the feelings convention demands, and society feels threatened.
js_115: He pushes away the comfort of an afterlife and holds only to the certainty of this life now. It is his final revolt against false hope.
js_116: The reconciliation Meursault reaches at the end. As the universe is indifferent, so he opens himself to the world and embraces the absurd.
js_118: The Stranger, together with The Myth of Sisyphus and Caligula, forms Camus’s ‘absurd’ cycle. It is philosophy written as a novel.
js_120: The funeral — the son who won’t cry
js_121: Meursault gets word of his mother’s death from the Marengo home and heads to the funeral.
js_122: Even during the vigil he drinks milk coffee, smokes, and stays perfectly unmoved.
js_123: Not a single tear — he doesn’t even know his mother’s exact age.
js_124: His senses lead: he feels the heat and drowsiness more sharply than any grief.
js_126: Marie and Raymond — the everyday pulls him in
js_127: The day after the funeral he meets his old coworker Marie at the sea and becomes her lover.
js_128: They watch a comedy and laugh together, living as if yesterday’s funeral never happened.
js_129: At his neighbor Raymond’s request, he writes a letter to lure Raymond’s mistress back.
js_130: Alongside, old Salamano grieving his lost dog is layered into the scene.
js_132: The beach — a shot under the sun
js_133: They go out to the seaside cabin of Raymond’s friend Masson.
js_134: They clash with the mistress’s brother and his group, and Raymond is slashed with a knife.
js_135: Back at the beach alone, Meursault comes face to face with the blinding sun.
js_136: Crushed by light and sweat, he fires once — then four more times.
js_139: Arrested, Meursault is locked up and put through a preliminary hearing.
js_140: The examining magistrate thrusts a crucifix at him and demands repentance, but he stays indifferent.
js_141: He slowly grows used to the monotony of jail and loses his sense of time.
js_142: The magistrate calls the unrepentant man ‘the Antichrist.’
js_144: The court — the crime of not crying
js_145: The trial dwells less on the killing than on ‘not crying at the funeral.’
js_146: The prosecutor casts him as ‘a soulless man who killed his mother in his heart.’
js_147: Meursault himself is pushed out of the lead role in his own trial and becomes a bystander.
js_148: The verdict is death — beheading in a public square.
js_151: When the chaplain presses God and the afterlife on him, Meursault flies into a rage and shoves him away.
js_152: Instead of false comfort, he grips the certainty of this life, right now.
js_153: At last he accepts ‘the gentle indifference of the world’ and finds peace.
js_154: He hopes that on the day of his execution a crowd will greet him with cries of hatred.
js_155: Coffee and cigarettes at the vigil
js_156: On the night he keeps watch over his mother’s coffin, Meursault drinks milk coffee, smokes, and even dozes off. In themselves these are utterly trivial acts.
js_157: At the trial these tiny fragments are stitched together into evidence of ‘a soulless man.’ It makes you ask whether we condemn others on just a few impressions.
js_158: The sun that pulled the trigger
js_159: Meursault shoots the Arab not out of grudge or plan but crushed by the sun stabbing his eyes and the sweat on his brow. That the motive is sensation alone feels deeply strange.
js_160: It shows how meaningless the world can be in the face of a reasonless act. That the question ‘why?’ never gets an answer — that is the absurd.
js_161: A death sentence over a mother’s tears
js_162: The court fixates less on the shooting than on ‘not crying at the funeral,’ and the prosecutor uses it to secure a death sentence.
js_163: It reveals that what society punishes may be not the act but the breach of convention. How much can we really tolerate ‘someone who feels differently’?
js_164: Should you fake sadness you don’t feel? Is refusing to dress up feelings you don’t have honesty — or a fault?
js_165: Meursault simply declined to act out false feeling. Think about the line between what we call ‘manners’ and ‘lies.’
js_166: If the world answers with no meaning at all, how are we to live?
js_167: Camus says to neither look away from that meaninglessness nor despair at it, but to embrace it. Think about why a ‘gentle indifference’ can be a comfort.
js_168: Why can’t society bear ‘someone who feels differently from us’?
js_169: What made Meursault a stranger was his difference, more than the murder. Ask yourself who the ‘stranger’ beside us is today.
js_171: You read the novel down to its corners
js_175: You’ve got the skeleton — one more round!

_app.title: The Stranger
_app.desc: The story of a man who won’t even fake his grief — read Camus’s The Stranger through a dopamine repeat quiz.
